coredata.dc:title: Si,N co-doped carbon quantum dots in mesoporous molecular sieves: A fluorescence sensing platform for Cr(VI) detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jing, Cai
Liu, Zisheng
Liu, Sicheng
Li, Bihao
Li, Xinhuan
Hu, Jiabao
Wang, Binsong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are highly efficient fluorescent probes for metal ion detection; however, they are limited by non-uniform particle size, aggregation, and the inability to facilitate quantitative analysis. Herein, a smart host–guest strategy is utilized, wherein CQDs are immobilized in mesoporous molecular sieves, proving to be an effective method to minimize these drawbacks. Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is successfully synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal route using Si,N-CQDs precursor materials as guests and the mesoporous molecular sieve SBA-15 as the host. A good linear relationship between the concentration of Cr(VI) ions and fluorescent intensity is observed from 0 μM to 100 μM (R 2 = 0.9986). Thelimitofdetection(LOD) is 0.213 μM, approximately three times lower than that of pristine Si,N-CQDs (0.677 μM). Additionally, Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 exhibit good detection selectivity for Cr(VI) detection over other metal ions. Moreover, a smartphone-based laboratory device and RGB analysis app are employed to capture and analyze fluorescence images, revealing a LOD for Cr(VI) of 0.201 μM. Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) is identified as the possible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 by Cr(VI) through UV–vis and fluorescence experiments. The practicability of the synthesized fluorescent probes is further validated in river water, yielding satisfactory spiked recoveries ranging from 99.24 % to 106.21 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Si,N-CQDs
SBA-15
Host-guest strategy
Selective detection of Cr(VI)
RGB analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 to Cr(VI)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process (a) of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; SAXRD patterns (b) of SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; FT-IR spectra (c) of SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; BET patterns of (d) SBA-15 and (e) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15.
SEM of (a) SBA-15 and (b) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; Particle size distribution of (c) CQDs and (d) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15.
Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 EDS mapping of (a-d) C, N, O and Si.
TEM of (a) SBA-15 and (b) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 (Illustration: Si,N-CQDs corresponding HRTEM images).
TGA of (a) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; XPS spectrum of (b) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; High-resolution XPS spectra (c-f) of C1s, N1s, O1s, and Si2p spectra.
The excitation-emission spectrum (a) of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; Storage time on fluorescence intensity (b) of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 strength.
(a) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 relative fluorescence with 500 µM metal ions; (b) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 + Cr(VI) system relative fluorescence with 500 µM various anions;

The fluorescence emission spectrum of (a) Si,N-CQDs and (c) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; The relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of (b) Si,N-CQDs and (d) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 was added with different concentrations of Cr(VI) (Illustration: Linear correlation between the ratio of F0/F and the concentration of Cr(VI)).

The fluorescence emission spectrum of (a) K-CQDs@SBA-15 and (c) P-CQDs@SBA-15; The relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) of (b) K-CQDs@SBA-15 and (d) P-CQDs@SBA-15 was added with different concentrations of Cr(VI) (Illustration: Linear correlation between the ratio of F0/F and the concentration of Cr(VI)).

(a) Absorption and excitation-emission spectra of Cr(VI) and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; (b) UV–Visible absorption spectra of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 + Cr(VI); (c) Fluorescence lifetimes of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 in the presence and absence of Cr(VI).

(a) Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 with adds Cr(VI) quench diagram; (b) Applet interface; (c) Reproducibility of the Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 response to Cr(VI) with 3 µM; (d) Curve graph of RGB values with different concentrations of Cr(VI) (Illustration: Linear range of RGB values with different Cr(VI) concentration (0–100 µM)); (e) Images of collected Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 at various concentrations of Cr(VI).
Numerical analysis and comparison of SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15.
Comparison with other fluorescent probes for detecting Cr(VI).
Detection of Cr(VI) by using Si,N-CDs@SBA-15 in river water.

head.ce:title: Si,N co-doped carbon quantum dots in mesoporous molecular sieves: A fluorescence sensing platform for Cr(VI) detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Nanotechnology, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are highly efficient fluorescent probes for metal ion detection; however, they are limited by non-uniform particle size, aggregation, and the inability to facilitate quantitative analysis. Herein, a smart host–guest strategy is utilized, wherein CQDs are immobilized in mesoporous molecular sieves, proving to be an effective method to minimize these drawbacks. Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is successfully synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal route using Si,N-CQDs precursor materials as guests and the mesoporous molecular sieve SBA-15 as the host. A good linear relationship between the concentration of Cr(VI) ions and fluorescent intensity is observed from 0 μM to 100 μM (R 2 = 0.9986). Thelimitofdetection(LOD) is 0.213 μM, approximately three times lower than that of pristine Si,N-CQDs (0.677 μM). Additionally, Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 exhibit good detection selectivity for Cr(VI) detection over other metal ions. Moreover, a smartphone-based laboratory device and RGB analysis app are employed to capture and analyze fluorescence images, revealing a LOD for Cr(VI) of 0.201 μM. Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) is identified as the possible mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 by Cr(VI) through UV–vis and fluorescence experiments. The practicability of the synthesized fluorescent probes is further validated in river water, yielding satisfactory spiked recoveries ranging from 99.24 % to 106.21 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data used in this study are publicly available.

ce:section.ce:para: In aquatic environments, chromium exists in two forms: trivalent chromium (Cr(III)), which is used as an additive for animal growth and metabolism [1], and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)), posing significant risks to human health such as carcinogenicity and teratogenicity. Its high mobility also constitutes a clear threat to the environment [2]. Therefore, accurately analyzing Cr(VI) in water samples is crucial [3]. Several techniques have been explored for chromium analysis in environmental samples, including voltammetry [4], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS)[5,6], ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis) [7], and X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy [8]. These detection methods are relatively complex and expensive. In contrast, CQDs based on quasi-spherical nanoparticles as fluorescent nanoprobes offer significant advantages such as low cost, strong chemical inertness, simple preparation, high photochemical stability, good biocompatibility, and low cellular toxicity [9–13].

CQDs mainly comprise graphene or graphene oxide sheets with sp2 hybridized carbon, and their conjugated structure facilitates excellent electron transfer and storage properties [14]. Most untreated CQDs do not achieve the expected levels of fluorescence and quantum yields (QY) [15], limiting their potential for widespread practical applications across various fields. Surface functionalization and heteroatom doping effectively enhance their fluorescence performance [16]. Heteroatom-doped CQDs, such as N, P, S, B, and Se doping, have been reported [17–21]. As a member of Group IV elements, silicon possesses good biocompatibility and environmental friendliness, making it a promising material for CQDs doping. Qian and Gao et al. introduced silicon elements during the carbonization process of CQDs to modulate the characteristics and performance of the CQDs [22,23]. Liu et al. introduced silane and organic polycarboxylic acid during the synthesis of CQDs to achieve the organic silane functionalization of CQDs [24]. Talited et al. achieved the organic silane functionalization of CQDs by employing silane coupling agents during the citric acid pyrolysis process [25]. Due to the many silane chains on the surface of CQDs, the single particle is maintained without aggregation and self-absorption in an aqueous solution [26]. Moreover, doping CQDs with N atoms is an efficient strategy for improving their photocatalytic and fluorescent properties.

Nevertheless, CQDs typically suffer from aggregation-induced luminescence quenching, resulting in weak or quenched photoluminescence in the solid state. Consequently, most sensing platforms based on CQDs are utilized in solution, presenting challenges for quantification and inconvenience for solid-state applications [27]. To address these, various solid carriers, including polymer matrices, inorganic salts, and porous materials (PMs), have been proposed for immobilizing CQDs and preparing CQD-based nanocomposites. These carriers aim to overcome aggregation issues in the solid state, such as fluorescence quenching, surface defects, and reduced surface area [28]. PMs characterized by their multi-purpose porous structures, offer nanoconfined space for generating uniform-sized CQDs, unique stabilization capabilities, and the ability to interact with guest materials, thus addressing the challenge of difficult separation. Consequently, PMs represent an ideal matrix for loading CQDs [29].

Many studies have focused on using porous TiO2 as a CQDs carrier and introducing CQDs in synthesizing porous materials. Zhao et al. mixed TiO2 with CQDs in proportion to obtain a mixture and then placed it into a tube Muffle furnace to yield brown CQDs/TiO2 [30]. Chang et al. mixed P25 and N,S-CQDs solution to prepare N,S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites by hydrothermal reaction [31]. However, the size distribution of synthesized particles was non-uniform, with most floating on the surface and only a small fraction entering the channels. This reduced fluorescence efficiency affected the Cr(VI) detection. The pore diameter of microporous structures is less than 2 nm, and the pore diameter of macroporous structures is between 50 nm and a few µm, and they are not suitable for limited domain CQDs. Therefore, mesoporous molecular sieve SBA-15 was employed as a nanoreactor to enable the Si,N-CQDs precursor to grow within its pore channels, effectively eliminating the above problems. Mesoporous SBA-15 material is characterized by a narrow pore size distribution, highly ordered two-dimensional hexagonal structure (p6 mm), large pore size (5–30 nm), and thick pore walls (3–9 nm). Compared with other mesoporous materials, SBA-15 exhibits higher thermal and hydrothermal stability. Therefore, it is a suitable host matrix to accommodate small functional species [32,33]. Additionally, it does not interfere with the CQDs excitation and emission due to the optical transparency of silica [34].

In comparison with majority of synthesis methods where preformed CQDs were simply mixed with PMs through impregnation reactions resulting in poor thermal stability and non-uniform sizes of CQDs [35], a smart host–guest synthesis strategy where the in situ fabrication of CQDs in the mesoporous material serves SBA-15 was proposed by our group. Besides resulting in uniform or tunable sizes of CQDs, it also provides an ideal rigid microenvironment for CQDs, thus avoiding the photoluminescence quenching effect induced by CQDs aggregation.

Here, a fluorescence sensing platform is constructed for on-site real-time detection of Cr(VI). Firstly, the selectivity and anti-interference ability of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 towards Cr(VI) detection are investigated, and the detection limit of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 for Cr(VI) is compared with that of Si,N-CQDs. Secondly, other types of CQDs are also confined to SBA-15, and their Cr(VI) detection performance is evaluated. Thirdly, the mechanism of Cr(VI) quenching Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is proposed based on characterization and experimental results. Finally, rapid image capture is conducted using a smartphone app developed to recognize RGB values based on color changes, enabling visual quantification of the target analyte.
Chemical and reagent details are given in the Supplementary Material.

0.6 g P123 was dissolved in 12 mL HCl (2 M) under continuous stirring at 45 °C until the solution became clear and transparent (Fig. 1 a). Then, 0.1 g CTAB was dissolved in 6 mL water and 5 mL ethanol. Both solutions were vigorously shaken at room temperature. Then, 2 mL TEOS was slowly added dropwise while stirring at 40 °C for 30 min. The resulting solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reaction vessel and heated at 80 °C for 5 h, followed by incubation at 120 °C for 12 h. The solid product was calcined at 550 °C for 5 h at a heating and cooling rate of 5 °C/min.

0.2 mL KH-550 and 0.2 g β-cyclodextrin were mixed in 30 mL deionized water till complete dissolution. After this, 0.1 g spherical SBA-15 was added, stirred for 3 h, and then sonicated for 1 h. The resulting mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reaction vessel and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After centrifugal drying of the samples, the final product obtained was blue fluorescent Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15.
Details of the characterization for Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 are provided in the Supplementary Material.
Experimental details for Cr(VI) detection are described in the Supplementary Material.

For the quantitative analysis based on smartphones, 4 mL of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 solution was added to the cuvette. Additionally, 1 mL of various Cr(VI) concentrations was mixed in. After equilibrating for 3-minute, the fluorescence images were obtained under 365 nm UV light excitation. The captured image was analyzed directly through a smartphone app, and the sum of RGB values was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
Sum
R G B
v a l u e s
=
R e d + G r e e n + B l u e

The crystal structures of SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 are analyzed by small-angle X-ray diffraction (SAXRD). The SAXRD spectrum of SBA-15 exhibits well-resolved diffraction peaks corresponding to the (100) plane, confirming its ordered hexagonal mesoporous structure (Fig. 1b). Compared to traditional rod-shaped SBA-15, the peaks of (110) and (200) in spherical SBA-15 are missing. Additionally, as the molar ratio of P123/CTAB decreases, the numerical value of the d-spacing of the (100) plane also decreases, resulting in the (100) peak not being prominent [36]. The characteristic peaks disappear by incorporating Si,N-CQDs into SBA-15, indicating the loss of ordered mesoporous structure. This may be attributed to the widespread distribution within the channels of SBA-15, disrupting the mesopore-ordered mesoporous structure [37].

SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 surface chemical compositions are analyzed by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Fig. 1c illustrates that SBA-15 exhibits distinct absorption peaks at 471.13 cm−1 and 1079.07 cm−1 assigned to the Si-O-Si bands of the inorganic framework [38,39]. The peak at 1636.67 cm−1 is attributed to the N-H groups bending vibration, and the peak at 3377.97 cm−1 is associated with the O-H groups stretching vibrations. The characteristic absorption band at 795.35 cm−1 is assigned to the Si-O groups [40,41]. A detailed comparison of the characteristic peaks of SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 shows two additional peaks in Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 at 2939.69 cm−1 and 578.74 cm−1, corresponding to C-H groups of Si,N-CQDs and benzene ring, respectively. These absorbance peaks indicate the presence of Si,N-CQDs on the SBA-15 surface [42].

The prepared SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 are also characterized for nitrogen adsorption–desorption analysis (Table 1 ). SBA-15 exhibits an I-type isotherm and an H4 hysteresis loop, indicating mesopores with a uniform pore size distribution, predominantly at 38 nm (Fig. 1d). Moreover, the specific surface area (SBET) is 107.35 m2/g, and the pore volume (Vp) is 0.8351 m3/g. Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 shows an III-type isotherm and an H3 hysteresis loop (Fig. 1e), suggesting the lack of ordered mesoporous structure, with pore sizes distribution of 14 nm, SBET of 11.05 m2/g, and Vp of 0.1464 m3/g. The significant decrease in specific surface area after incorporating Si,N-CQDs into SBA-15 indicates the blockage of mesopores by Si,N-CQDs.

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) is employed to observe the morphology of SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15. Bare SBA-15 exhibits a distinct spherical structure, as illustrated in Fig. 2 a. After confining Si,N-CQDs within the SBA-15 network, the Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 morphology remains spherical (Fig. 2b). Subsequent particle size analysis of SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is shown in Fig. 2c and 2d, illustrating that the average particle size for both materials is around 0.62 μm. Adding Si,N-CQDs does not significantly alter the SBA-15 morphology, reflecting the structural stability of SBA-15, making it suitable as a nanoreactor for Si,N-CQDs.

The incorporation of Si,N-CQDs in SBA-15 is confirmed through energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and elemental mapping analysis. Fig. S1 shows that the EDS spectrum of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 confirmed the presence of C, N, O, and Si. Fig. 3 (a-d) depicts that elemental mapping analysis reveals the uniform C, N, O, and Si distribution in Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15. Notably, N is present in Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15; however, it is absent in SBA-15, indicating that the N source is Si,N-CQDs. EDS analysis suggests that the nitrogen content in Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is 3.7 wt%, and the calculated amount of Si,N-CQDs is 18.5 wt% based on the nitrogen content [43].

The morphology and microstructure of SBA-15 and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 are also observed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image of SBA-15 (Fig. 4 a) reveals mesoporous channels, a two-dimensional hexagonal P6mm structure, and a highly ordered hexagonal array. By incorporating Si,N-CQDs into SBA-15, nanoscale Si,N-CQDs particles with a size around (Fig. 4b Illustration) 8 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm are anchored on SBA-15, as shown in Fig. 4b. These results prove the successful synthesis of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 composite.

The Si,N-CQDs content in Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is determined through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), as illustrated in Fig. 5 a. TGA analyses of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15, and SBA-15 indicate that the initial weight loss at around 80 °C (3 %) is primarily attributed to the evaporation of physically adsorbed water molecules from the mesoporous surface and internal channels. The weight of SBA-15 remains unchanged with further temperature increases, while the covalently anchored organic components in Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 gradually decompose. The weight loss of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 in the temperature range of 100–800 °C is mainly attributed to carbon species combustion with oxygen-containing functional groups, reaching 38 %. The quantitative thermal analysis of Si,N-CQDs combined with EDS results indicates that Si,N-CQDs are present on the SBA-15 surface and within pores.

The elemental composition and chemical states of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 are investigated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as presented in Fig. 5b. The XPS spectrum of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 presented reveals four prominent peaks at 101.50, 286.18, 401.91, and 532.42 eV, corresponding to Si2p, O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. The illustration in Fig. 5b indicates elemental percentages of Si, O, N, and C, which are 20.60 %, 32.40 %, 4.59 %, and 42.41 %, respectively. After deconvolution, (Fig. 5c) the C1s spectrum resolves into three peaks at 284.25 eV (C=C), 285.93 eV (C-N), and 287.60 eV (C=O) [44]. The N1s (Fig. 5d) spectrum displays three peaks at 400.82, 398.82, and 406.22 eV, corresponding to C-N, N-H, and N-Si, respectively [45]. The deconvoluted O1s (Fig. 5e) spectrum shows three peaks at 530.85, 532.82, and 531.59 eV, attributed to C-O, C=O, and Si-O groups, indicating the oxygen-containing groups on Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 surface and emphasizing its diversity [46]. The Si2p (Fig. 5f) spectrum has three peaks at 104.59, 103.66, and 103.20 eV, corresponding to Si-N, Si-C, and Si-O [47]. The synthesized Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 possess abundant hydrophilic functional groups. These results confirm a deeper understanding of the surface chemical characteristics of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15.

The fluorescence characteristics of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 are depicted in Fig. 6 a, where bright blue light at 426 nm is emitted after UV light excitation at an optimal wavelength of 343 nm. Subsequently, the Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 fluorescence intensity is measured at different pH conditions (3 to 10). As shown in Fig. S2, it is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is highest at pH 7. The fluorescence intensity of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 decreases under acidic conditions due to the protonation of surface amino groups, which removes the unpaired electrons acting as electron donors within the amino groups, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity [48]. Under alkaline conditions, the remaining carboxyl groups (–COOH) in Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 undergo deprotonation (–COO-), and the charged electrons of the carboxyl groups can weaken the electron-attracting properties of the carboxylic acid groups in Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15, thereby affecting its fluorescence intensity [49]. Due to the excellent fluorescence performance of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 under neutral conditions, its suitability for practical water sample detection. Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is stored under ambient conditions for 7 weeks, with fluorescence intensity measured weekly to investigate the optical stability. There is no significant decrease in fluorescence intensity over the observation period, suggesting excellent photostability of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 (Fig. 6b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 to Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Relevant experiments are conducted to investigate the selectivity of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 for metal ions. Fluorescence spectra of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 are recorded after adding various metal ions, and their impact on the fluorescence intensity is observed. Thirteen common metal ions, including Ag+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Cd2+, and Cr(VI), are selected each at 500 μM concentration. Cr(VI) exhibits the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency on Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15, as shown in Fig. 7 a. These results indicate that Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 possess excellent selectivity for Cr(VI), which strongly supports its potential application in sensing Cr(VI) ions. A similar quenching experiment is conducted by adding different metal ions to assess the anti-interference ability of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 for Cr(VI) detection. The material exhibits superior performance for detecting Cr(VI) ions even in the presence of other metal ions (Fig. 7b). This experimental result suggests that adding interfering ions does not affect the fluorescence quenching, providing a basis for detecting Cr(VI) in natural water samples.

To validate the accuracy of Cr(VI) detection by Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15, the detection limits are compared before and after incorporating Si,N-CQDs into SBA-15. As shown in Fig. S3, Si,N-CQDs demonstrate specific recognition of Cr(VI). The fluorescence properties of Si,N-CQDs, as depicted in Fig. S4, exhibit bright blue emission at 440 nm upon excitation with ultraviolet light at the optimal wavelength of 350 nm. The absorption peak of Cr(VI) coincides precisely with the excitation wavelength of Si,N-CQDs at 350 nm (Fig. S5). Therefore, the excitation wavelength of Si,N-CQDs can be absorbed by Cr(VI), leading to effective quenching of the fluorescence of Si,N-CQDs by Cr(VI). Si,N-CQDs are capable of specifically recognizing Cr(VI). Trend of fluorescence intensity of Si,N-CQDs with varying Cr(VI) concentrations (Fig. 8 a), surpassing 80 % fluorescence quenching efficiency at a concentration of 500 μM. Further determination involves linear fitting of fluorescence intensity data (F0/F) against Cr(VI) concentration. Fig. 8b demonstrates that the concentration range for the fluorescence response of Si,N-CQDs solution to Cr(VI) is 0 to 100 μM. Additionally, F0/F exhibits a linear relationship with Cr(VI) concentration, Y = 0.0057x + 1.0104 (R 2 = 0.9944). The detection limit is calculated using the formula 3σ/M, by conducting linear regression analysis on the change in fluorescence intensity with Cr(VI) concentration, the slope ‘M’ of the linear equation was obtained. Simultaneously, through statistical analysis of fluorescence intensity data, the standard deviation σ of the fluorescence intensity was calculated. Substituting these two values into the formula, the detection limit for Si,N-CQDs towards Cr(VI) was calculated to be 0.677 μM. Similarly, Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 undergoes corresponding testing (Fig. 8c). With increased Cr(VI) concentration, the Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 fluorescence intensity gradually decreases. The linear fitting of fluorescence intensity data F0/F against Cr(VI) concentration is shown in Fig. 8d. The fluorescence reaction concentration interval for Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 solution to Cr(VI) 0 to 100 μM, and Y = 0.0012x + 1.0104 (R 2 = 0.9986), with a detection limit of 0.213 μM. Adding SBA-15 improves the detection performance, emphasizing the advantages of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 as a fluorescence sensing platform for highly sensitive and selective Cr(VI) detection. This highlights the potential application prospects of proposed sensing probes in environmental monitoring and other fields.

The reliability of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is validated by adding standard samples to real water samples and determining the recovery rates. After centrifugation through a 0.22 μm organic membrane, the River water samples are directly used for the spiking recovery experiments. The recovery rates range from 99.24 % to 106.21 % (Table 3 ), suggesting that the established method is highly reliable for environmental water samples.

A series of measurements are conducted to investigate the potential fluorescence quenching mechanism induced by Cr(VI) on Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15. The UV–visible absorption spectra of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15, Cr(VI), and Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 + Cr(VI) were observed. The UV absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) significantly coincides with the Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 10 a), reflecting that the quenching mechanism involves Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) or Inner Filter Effect (IFE) [52,53]. However, static quenching necessitates strong interactions and ground-state complex formation between the fluorophore and quencher, often changing the absorption spectra [54]. Adding Cr(VI) induces in a redshift in the UV–Visible absorption peaks of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 (Fig. 10b). This shift is attributed to the interaction between surface functional groups of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15, and Cr(VI) without forming ground-state complexes. This undoubtedly excludes static quenching, confirming a plausible FRET mechanism [55]. The Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 fluorescence lifetimes are measured at 343 nm in the presence and absence of Cr(VI), as depicted in Fig. 10c, to validate the FRET mechanism further. The Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 fluorescence lifetime is 4.79 ns, while that of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 + Cr(VI) is 3.10 ns [56]. The decrease in fluorescence lifetime serves as additional confirmation that the quenching mechanism of Cr(VI) on Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 is indeed FRET.

Due to their cumbersome operation and limitations imposed by experimental instruments, traditional fluorescence detection methods highlight challenges in image acquisition and data analysis for portable on-site monitoring. Therefore, a portable and integrated device is developed for quantitatively analyzing metal ions using Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15. The device is equipped with a 100-milliwatt mini UV light, emitting a wavelength of 365 nm as the excitation light source. To resolve the image acquisition challenge, fluorescence images are captured using a smartphone camera. An application developed independently and capable of identifying RGB values in the images controls the smartphone and acquires corresponding RGB values to resolve the image data analysis challenge. This process enhances the efficiency and operability of portable on-site monitoring, particularly in the quantitative analysis of metal ions (Video S1).

The following experiments are conducted to verify the unique, comprehensive advantages of the portable handheld device in terms of detection limit and detection time. Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 emits blue fluorescence under UV excitation, and the fluorescence is quenched upon adding Cr(VI) due to FRET (Fig. 11 a). The smartphone camera could capture photo images of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 solutions at different Cr(VI) concentrations, as depicted in Fig. 11e. Blind repeated measurements of the fluorescence probe response in Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 + Cr(VI) (3 μM) solution yields a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.11 % (n = 6), indicating good repeatability (Fig. 11c). The digital images RGB values are transformed into corresponding color scale cards and assessed through software (Fig. 11b). Fig. 11d depicts the relationship between the RGB values of the analyzed image and the concentration of Cr(VI). The detection limit is 0.201 µM within the linear range of 0–100 µM. These results demonstrate that the handheld smartphone-based device provides a portable on-site strategy for quantitative Cr(VI) monitoring in environmental samples.

The prepared Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 exhibits stable photoluminescent properties and effectively eliminates self-quenching issues resulting from the solid-state aggregation of Si,N-CQDs. The solid state of Si,N-CQDs cannot exhibit photoluminescence, while the fluorescence efficiency of Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 remains above 95 % after 7 weeks of storage. Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 demonstrates high sensitivity and selectivity in detecting Cr(VI), with a low detection limit (0.213 µM) and a wide linear range. Additionally, the Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 fluorescence quenching mechanism is confirmed to be FRET. Subsequently, other CQDs types are also confined into SBA-15 and compared with the literature, with detection limits of 0.866 µM and 0.314 µM, confirming the feasibility of the proposed strategy. Moreover, a portable smartphone fluorescence sensor is created using the Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 system. This system achieves a low detection limit of 0.201 µM by analyzing RGB values. It allows for quick, convenient, and on-site quantification of Cr(VI) in real samples. Therefore, the portable device utilizing Si,N-CQDs@SBA-15 as a fluorescence probes-based portable devices offers a promising detection solution for environmental pollution monitoring.

All authors contributed to the design, experimental and measurement processes, as well as the preparation of this manuscript. Furthermore, all authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.

Cai Jing: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Zisheng Liu: Writing – review & editing. Sicheng Liu: Writing – review & editing. Bihao Li: Writing – review & editing. Xinhuan Li: Writing – review & editing. Jiabao Hu: Writing – review & editing. Binsong Wang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110726.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. He, S. Qi, Z. Sun, G. Zhu, K. Zhang, W. Chen, Si, N-codoped carbon dots: preparation and application in iron overload diagnosis, Journal of Materials Science 54 (2019) 4297–4305.
